result:
:::writing
The train arrived without ceremony, as if it had always been there and only just now allowed itself to be noticed. It hummed at the platform with a soft, patient energy, doors open, lights warm, the faint scent of metal and rain drifting out into the evening air. No announcements echoed, no crowds gathered, and yet the timetable on the wall insisted, with stubborn confidence, that this was the 7:12 to Somewhere You Meant to Go.

Mara stood beneath the flickering clock and wondered, not for the first time, how many versions of her life she had almost lived. There were so many small decisions that had seemed insignificant at the time: a job declined, a call not returned, a suitcase unpacked too soon. Each one had quietly closed a door somewhere, even as others opened with equal quietness. Life, she had learned, rarely made a sound when it changed.

The train conductor leaned halfway out of a window and gave her a look that wasn’t quite impatience and wasn’t quite kindness. “You getting on,” he asked, “or are you waiting for a better version of this moment?”

Mara laughed, because the question was both absurd and uncomfortably precise. “Is that what this is?” she asked. “A moment?”

“What else would it be?” he replied, then disappeared back inside without waiting for an answer.

She stepped forward, then hesitated. The platform stretched behind her into a dim corridor of possibilities, all the familiar exits leading back to the life she knew. The train, by contrast, offered no guarantees—only the quiet promise of movement. She had always found it easier to stay still and imagine change than to actually step into it.

But something about the stillness of the platform unsettled her. It wasn’t peaceful. It was suspended, like a breath held too long.

She boarded.

Inside, the train felt larger than it had any right to be. The narrow doorway opened into a series of carriages that curved gently out of sight, each one lit in a slightly different hue. The first glowed with a golden, late-afternoon warmth; the next flickered with cooler, bluish tones like early morning. Further down, she glimpsed shadows and something that might have been candlelight.

There were people, but not many. A man sat by the window reading a book that had no title on its cover. A child pressed her nose to the glass, watching a landscape that didn’t seem to move. An elderly woman held a bundle of yarn that unraveled slowly into nothing, her hands knitting a pattern that never quite completed.

Mara chose a seat near the middle, unsure whether proximity to either end mattered. The train doors closed with a soft sigh, and for a moment, nothing happened.

Then, without any sense of acceleration, the world outside shifted.

It didn’t blur like scenery usually does. Instead, it changed in discrete, deliberate scenes, as though someone were turning the pages of a book. A city street became a forest path, which became a shoreline, which became a narrow alley lined with shuttered shops. Each image lingered just long enough to feel real before giving way to the next.

“First time?” the man with the untitled book asked, without looking up.

“Yes,” Mara said. “Is it that obvious?”

“Everyone asks questions at the beginning,” he replied. “Later, they just watch.”

“What is this place?” she asked.

He turned a page that seemed blank. “A way through,” he said. “Not necessarily forward. Not always backward. Just… through.”

Mara frowned. “That doesn’t explain anything.”

“It explains enough,” he said, and returned to his reading.

She leaned back in her seat and watched the shifting world outside. There was something hypnotic about it, something that invited her to let go of the need to understand. For a while, she did just that.

Then she saw it.

The scene outside had settled into something more stable—a small café on a quiet street, its windows glowing against the evening. Inside, a younger version of Mara sat at a corner table, stirring a cup of coffee she never intended to drink. Across from her sat someone she hadn’t thought about in years.

They were laughing.

Not the polite laughter of acquaintances, but the kind that spills out unguarded, the kind that assumes there will be more moments just like it. Mara felt a tightening in her chest as she watched the scene unfold. She remembered this. Or rather, she remembered not realizing how important it was at the time.

“I could have stayed,” she murmured.

The man across the aisle spoke without lifting his gaze. “You could have done many things.”

“Do you see it too?” she asked.

He shook his head. “No. I see my own versions.”

Mara pressed her hand against the window, as if she could reach through it. “Can it be changed?” she asked.

“No,” he said simply.

“Then what’s the point of showing it to me?”

This time, he looked up. His expression wasn’t unkind, but it was steady in a way that made it difficult to argue with. “To show you that it exists,” he said. “Or existed. Or might have existed. The point depends on what you do with it now.”

Mara sat back, the café fading into another scene before she could respond. This time it was something she didn’t recognize—a place she had never been, a life she had never imagined. She saw herself again, older, standing on a hillside beneath a sky painted with unfamiliar stars. There was a sense of quiet satisfaction in that version of her, a calm she struggled to recognize as her own.

“Is that… real?” she asked.

“Potentially,” the man said.

“That’s not very comforting.”

“It’s not meant to be.”

The train continued its silent journey, carrying its passengers through fragments of what was, what could have been, and what might still be. Mara realized, slowly, that none of the scenes lingered long enough to fully inhabit, and none disappeared so quickly that they felt meaningless. They existed in a careful balance, inviting reflection but resisting attachment.

Eventually, the conductor returned, moving through the carriage with unhurried steps. He stopped beside Mara’s seat and regarded her with that same almost-smile.

“Well?” he asked. “Is it what you expected?”

“I didn’t know what to expect,” she said. “I’m still not sure what this is.”

“That’s fine,” he replied. “Understanding is optional. Participation isn’t.”

Mara glanced toward the window again, where a new scene was forming—something indistinct, something not yet clear enough to recognize.

“Where does the train stop?” she asked.

The conductor tilted his head slightly, as if considering how to phrase the answer. “It doesn’t,” he said. “Not really. But you can get off whenever you like.”

“And then what?”

He smiled, just a little. “Then you’ll find out what this ride was for.”

He moved on before she could ask anything else, leaving her alone with the quiet rhythm of the train and the shifting world beyond the glass.

Mara exhaled slowly, feeling the weight of the question settle into something less urgent, more patient. She didn’t need to solve it immediately. Perhaps she never would.

For now, she watched.

And for the first time in a long while, that felt like enough.
:::writing

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
